coredata.dc:title: One-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules on periodically wrinkled graphene sheet: A Monte Carlo approach
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Sun-Young
Hwang, Ho Jung
Kang, Jeong Won
coredata.dc:description: Periodically wrinkled graphene sheet is of interest as a building block to develop nanoelectronic devices. This work presents that periodically wrinkled graphene sheet can be applied to a pattern, to form one-dimensionally well-ordered C60 molecules, via Monte Carlo simulations using the data obtained from atomistic calculations. Since the valleys of a sinusoidal graphene surface provide energetic ground sites for absorbed C60 molecules, their motions seeking stable positions lead to one-dimensional self-assembly. The size of the wrinkles, the density of adsorbed C60 molecules, and the temperature are very important parameters to obtain a one-dimensional C60 molecules array. We estimate high one-dimensional diffusion coefficients of C60 molecules on the wrinkled graphene surface. Our results can provide a possible approach to make a quantum information array, based on endohedral fullerenes and a graphene quantum dot array, by transforming C60 molecules to graphene nanoflakes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Wrinkled surface
C60/graphene
Monte Carlo simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Periodic potential energy surface of sinusoidal graphene sheet
One-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules: Monte Carlo simulations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) When a graphene sheet is transferred onto a sinusoidal substrate surface, by the adhesion of graphene on the substrate due to their attractive forces, (b) periodic graphene morphology with a sinusoidal wave can be formed on the surface.
Model structure of a sinusoidal graphene sheet.
Optimized C60ʼs positions versus the sinusoidal graphene surface and the corresponding vdW potential energies.
Binding energy of a C60 molecule on a sinusoidal graphene sheet and a Lorentz function to fit the potential energy surface.
Results of the Monte Carlo simulation for 100 C60 molecules at 200 K when the wavelength and the amplitude of the sinusoidal graphene surface were 12 nm and 2 nm, respectively.
Results of the Monte Carlo simulation for 100 C60 molecules at 200 K when the wavelength and the amplitude of the sinusoidal graphene surface were 6 nm and 2 nm, respectively.
Results of the Monte Carlo simulation for 200 C60 molecules at 200 K when the wavelength and the amplitude of the sinusoidal graphene surface were 6 nm and 2 nm, respectively.
Nine structures with Monte Carlo steps to display the motions of 200 C60 molecules at 300 K.

R
n
2
〉 / 2 n as a function of the Monte Carlo step for different dimensions. The inset shows the
〈

〉

/

as a function of the Monte Carlo step, where
N
step
is the number of the Monte Carlo step.

head.ce:title: One-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules on periodically wrinkled graphene sheet: A Monte Carlo approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Computer Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Chung-Ang University, Seoul 156-756, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Transportation System Engineering, Graduate School of Transportation, Korea National University of Transportation, Uiwang-si, Gyeonggi-do 437-763, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Periodically wrinkled graphene sheet is of interest as a building block to develop nanoelectronic devices. This work presents that periodically wrinkled graphene sheet can be applied to a pattern, to form one-dimensionally well-ordered C60 molecules, via Monte Carlo simulations using the data obtained from atomistic calculations. Since the valleys of a sinusoidal graphene surface provide energetic ground sites for absorbed C60 molecules, their motions seeking stable positions lead to one-dimensional self-assembly. The size of the wrinkles, the density of adsorbed C60 molecules, and the temperature are very important parameters to obtain a one-dimensional C60 molecules array. We estimate high one-dimensional diffusion coefficients of C60 molecules on the wrinkled graphene surface. Our results can provide a possible approach to make a quantum information array, based on endohedral fullerenes and a graphene quantum dot array, by transforming C60 molecules to graphene nanoflakes.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of C60 [1], many studies have been performed, in order to characterize the binding and ordering of fullerenes on solid surfaces [2,3]. C60 attracts much interest, because it represents a class of relatively simple model structures for studying the interactions of gases with carbon materials [2,4]. The controlled self-assembly of complex organic molecular nanostructures on technologically important substrates offers a number of powerful approaches for the development of organic molecular-based devices, which possess functions such as rectifying, switching and sensing [3,5–15]. Fullerenes have attracted considerable attention in recent years, due to their potential in surface chemistry and nanotemplating [15], non-linear optics [15,16], single-molecule transistors [13,17], and in particular, molecular electronics, because of their tunable electronic properties, resulting in superconducting or semiconducting behavior [13,18,19]. The formation and characterization of fullerene adlayers on surfaces are of great interest from the fundamental and technological points of view because they provide valuable information about molecular interactions, and can lead to potential applications in existing technologies. The study of these surface-supported systems is important for future developments in molecular electronics, since they represent promising materials for applications in advanced nanopatterning, surface templating, molecular data storage, solar cells, sensors/molecular recognition and functional surfaces [13,15–22].

Depending on the substrate, single layers of C60 molecules are found in different geometrical arrangements, and different bonding and charge states [2]. Over the last decade STM has been used intensively for the study of C60 self-assembled layers on a variety of metal [23–38] and semiconductor [20,39–43] surfaces. On most surfaces, fullerene molecules self-assemble into close-packed monolayers with a hexagonal or quasi-hexagonal structure, and a molecule–molecule separation close to 1 nm, as observed in bulk C60 [20,23–27,29–40,43]. In some cases the formation of a C60 monolayer leads to an adsorbate-induced reconstruction of the substrate [24,30,32–38].

In particular, the one-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules on a surface is of particular interest for molecular electronics [2,44,45]. Tamai et al. [2] reported one-dimensional chains of C60 molecules on Cu(221) surface, which has nominal terrace width on the particular vicinal surface of 0.766 nm, being very close to the average diameter of a C60 molecule. They showed aligned C60 molecules in well-ordered one-dimensional chains, along the step direction of the substrate. However, their STM images actually showed quasi-hexagonal close-packed pattern, because the gap between the terraces is 4% more than the diameter of a C60 molecule [2].

Recently, graphene [46,47] is considered as an ideal candidate for nanoelectromechanical system (NEMS) devices, because it may play an important role as a component of more complex systems of nanotechnology, as well as displaying unusual electronic and mechanical properties [46–58]. In particular, the substrate-regulated graphene morphologies have been intensively investigated [59–67], and their results have shown the potential of periodic surface deformation of graphene sheet. Hicks et al. [67] presented that periodic graphene nanoribbon trenches enable a substantial electronic bandgap, by coating bi-layer graphene on silicon carbide nanometer-scale steps. Neek-Amal and Peeters [65] investigated some sinusoidal graphene morphologies as periodic deformed surfaces. When a graphene sheet is transferred onto a sinusoidal substrate surface, as shown in Fig. 1 (a), by the adhesion of graphene on the substrate due to their attractive forces, periodic graphene morphology with a sinusoidal wave can be formed on the surface, as shown in Fig. 1(b).

C60/graphene hybrid structures consisting of a stacked single graphene sheet and C60 monolayer were produced experimentally [68]. It was found that the intercalated C60 molecules can rotate in between graphene sheets. The C60 on graphene was considered as a model system to test the strength of the van der Waals (vdW) forces [69]. Mechanical stability, friction, and adhesion are among the physical properties that are strongly dependent on the vdW interactions for the nanoscale [69]. The motion of C60 molecules on a graphene has been considered in a number of studies [58,70–72].

Therefore, in this Letter, we numerically investigate the one-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules on sinusoidal graphene sheet, via a Monte Carlo approach. We reveal that the absorbed C60 molecules move toward the valleys of the sinusoidal surface, which possess the ground energy states, and finally, one-dimensional chains of C60 molecules self-assemble. Our results suggest that the periodically deformed graphene sheets have a wide range of applications in molecular nanotechnology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Periodic potential energy surface of sinusoidal graphene sheet
ce:section.ce:para: First, we calculated the vdW potential energy of a C60 molecule on a sinusoidal graphene sheet, in order to study their interactions. Fig. 2 shows the model structure of a sinusoidal graphene sheet. We considered a zigzag-edge graphene with a width of 2 nm and length of 12 nm composed of 650 carbon atoms, and then a sinusoidal graphene sheet was obtained from uniaxial compression, via an optimization scheme, namely the steepest descent (SD) method [73], which is the simplest of the gradient methods. At this time, the interactions between carbon atoms that formed covalent bonds of graphene were modeled using the Tersoff–Brenner potential [74,75], and the long range interactions of carbon were characterized according to the Lennard-Jones 12–6 (LJ12–6) potential, with the parameters that were given by Mao et al. [76], such as ε = 0.0042 eV and σ = 3.37 Å . The cutoff distance of the LJ12–6 potential was 20 Å. The peak-to-peak amplitude and the wavelength of the sinusoidal graphene sheet were 1 nm and 3.9 nm, respectively.

After the sinusoidal graphene sheet was initially relaxed without C60 molecules, a C60 molecule was deposited on its hilltop as shown in Fig. 2. After the relaxation on it hilltop, the position of the C60 molecule was displaced toward one of valleys by 0.001 nm, and then we obtained the locally optimized C60 positions on the fixed sinusoidal graphene sheet, via the optimization process using the SD method.

Fig. 3 shows the optimized C60ʼs positions versus the sinusoidal graphene surface, and the corresponding vdW potential energies. Typically, the optimized spacing between the C60 and the GNF has been well known as ∼ 0.65 nm
[59,70,77]. In this work, the spacings on the hilltop and the valley were 0.611 and 0.633 nm, respectively. The vdW binding energy of the C60 molecule on the hilltop was 0.5 eV, and this value is slightly less than the 0.738 eV for the C60 molecule on the flat graphene [77]. It is clear that its interacting area on the hilltop of the curved surface is less than that on the flat surface. This value of 0.5 eV on the hilltop is compatible to its binding energy value of 0.537 eV on the top of a (10, 10) CNT [77]. As the C60 molecule moved off the hilltop, its binding energy slightly increased. However, as the C60 molecule approached the valley, its binding energy rapidly increased, and then its binding energy at the bottom of the valley was 1.074 eV. The binding energy is the least on the hilltop but the greatest in the valley. The activation energy for the transition of valley-to-valley is 0.537 eV.

These results suggest that C60 molecules absorbed on the sinusoidal graphene sheet will finally become stabilized in one of the valleys. The vdW interactions between C60 molecules make them to bind to each other. Therefore, C60 molecules in a valley attract each other, but their motions are one-dimensionally restricted along the valley, and as a result, the absorbed C60 molecules will be observed as one-dimensional chains along the valleys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules: Monte Carlo simulations
ce:section.ce:para: The two most widely used methods for atomic-level modeling are Monte Carlo (MC) statistical mechanics and molecular dynamics (MD) [78]. MD is an accurate but computationally expensive method whereas the Monte Carlo method is more efficient to obtain equilibrium state [79]. Jorgensen and Tirado-Rives [79] showed that the MD calculations require 1.6–3.8 times more computer time to achieve the same level of convergence as the Monte Carlo simulations. Especially, in this work, to improve the computational efficiency, we deal with the C60 molecule as a single particle on the wrinkled graphene sheet. So the Monte Carlo method is more suitable in this work.

Using the potential energy surface of the sinusoidal graphene sheet, we performed Monte Carlo simulations. Firstly, we generated the sinusoidal graphene sheet. Secondly, we randomly generated a number of C60 molecules. Finally, we performed Monte Carlo simulations, to obtain numerical optimization. The binding energy between a C60 molecule and the sinusoidal graphene sheet was approximately modeled by a Lorentz function to fit the potential energy surface, as shown in Fig. 4 . The parameters of the Lorentz function were adjusted, according to the wavelength and the amplitude of the sinusoidal graphene sheets. The vdW energy between two C60 molecules was calculated by the LJ12–6 potential function. The binding energy and equilibrium spacing between two C60 molecules were 0.278 eV and 1.005 nm, respectively [77]. In our Monte Carlo simulations, the gap between the C60 molecule and the surface was maintained as 0.65 nm.

We generated the sinusoidal graphene sheet on a substrate of 78 nm × 78 nm. Fig. 5 shows the results of the Monte Carlo simulation for 100 C60 molecules at 200 K, when the wavelength and the amplitude of the sinusoidal graphene surface were 12 nm and 2 nm, respectively. Inset figures show the corresponding structures of the Monte Carlo steps. One hundred C60 molecules, which were initially randomly distributed, were in good order along the valleys, after 200 Monte Carlo steps.

To study the alignment of C60 molecules on a more wrinkled surface, we performed the Monte Carlo simulation for 100 C60 molecules at 200 K, when the wavelength and the amplitude of the sinusoidal graphene surface were 6 nm and 2 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6 . From the vdW energy variations, we can see that after 110 Monte Carlo steps, most of the C60 molecules settled in the valley. This result implied that on more wrinkled graphene sheet, C60 molecules can become one-dimensional self-assembled much more quickly.

To observe the effect due to the number of C60 molecules, we performed the Monte Carlo simulation for 200 C60 molecules at 200 K, with the surface structure of Fig. 6. Fig. 7 shows the vdW energy variations as a function of the Monte Carlo step, and the four structures. From this figure, we can see that 200 C60 molecules were settled in the valleys, after 100 Monte Carlo steps. Therefore, we can suggest that the one-dimensional self-assembly processes of C60 molecules on the sinusoidal graphene sheet influences on the density of adsorbed C60 molecules.

Fig. 8 shows nine structures with the Monte Carlo steps, to display the motions of 200 C60 molecules at 300 K. During the initial stages, all C60 molecules moved into the valleys, and then lots of small linear chains were formed. As the Monte Carlo step increased, various phenomena, such as the clustering, the dissolution, the diffusions of C60 molecules were observed and the average size of C60 molecules chains increased.

The diffusion coefficients (D) of C60 molecules can be calculated according to Einsteinʼs formula using the mean square displacements of their center of mass, as follows [80]
(1)
D =
1
2 n
lim
t → ∞
∑
i = 1
N
|
R
i
( t ) −
R
i
( 0 ) |
2
t
.
Here, n is the degree of freedom, N is the number of C60 molecules, and
R
i
( t ) is the position of the ith C60 molecule at time t. In our Monte Carlo simulations, the elapsed time t cannot be directly evaluated. However, since the Monte Carlo step is almost linearly proportional to the elapsed time t [80], we can estimate the trend of the diffusion coefficients as a function of the Monte Carlo step. So Eq. (1) can be re-expressed by D t = 〈
R
n
2
〉 / 2 n , where 〈
R
n
2
〉 =
lim
t → ∞
∑
i = 1
N
|
R
i
( t ) −
R
i
( 0 ) |
2
[70].

Fig. 9 shows the 〈
R
n
2
〉 / 2 n as a function of the Monte Carlo step for different dimensions. The 〈
R
n
2
〉 / 2 n plots can be divided into three regions. In order to observe the trend of the diffusion coefficients, the inset in Fig. 9 shows the
〈
R
n
2
〉
2 n
/
N
step
as a function of the Monte Carlo step, where
N
step
is the number of the Monte Carlo step. First, the absorbed C60 molecules rapidly moved toward the valleys; hence, the 〈
R
n
2
〉 / 2 n rapidly increased, and the diffusion coefficients were also increased, as denoted by the ‘1’ region in Fig. 9. Second, C60 molecules in the bottoms of the valleys attracted each other at close range, and a number of small linear chains were formed in each valley. In this region denoted by ‘2’ in Fig. 9, the 〈
R
n
2
〉 / 2 n slightly increased; that is, the diffusion coefficients gradually decreased during the clustering progresses of C60 molecules. After that, some of the small linear chains were dissolved, and then free C60 molecules swiftly moved along the valley, and were aggregated with another chain. These phenomena resulted in the average size of the C60 chains increasing, whereas the number of small chains decreased. The 〈
R
n
2
〉 / 2 n increased in region ‘3’ more than in region ‘2’. Hence, the slopes in region ‘3’ were more than those in region ‘2’. The diffusion coefficients in region ‘3’ were almost maintained constant, and this region can be recognized as a steady state.

In regions ‘1’ and ‘2’, three-dimensional diffusion coefficients of C60 molecules were very similar to their two-dimensional diffusion coefficients. However, in region ‘3’, the former was less than the latter, because most of the C60 molecules moved along the bottom of the valleys. Therefore, their one-dimensional diffusion coefficients along the valleys were always greater than the other, because one-dimensional movements along the valleys were dominant in the motions of C60 molecules.

In kinetic lattice Monte Carlo simulations, the elapse time for a hopping event has been ∼
10
− 15
s , the reverse of the atomic vibrational frequency [80]. Considering the oscillation frequency of ∼ 1 THz between C60 molecules [81], the hopping time of C60 molecules can be approximated by ∼
10
− 12
s . When we used the value of 1 nm as the diameter of C60 molecule, the estimated diffusion coefficients of one-, two-, and three-dimensions after 106 Monte Carlo steps were 2 ×
10
− 8
, 1.1 ×
10
− 8
, and 7 ×
10
− 9
m
2
s
− 1
, respectively. Neek-Amal et al. [70] estimated the diffusion coefficient of 7 ×
10
− 10
m
2
s
− 1
for a C60 molecule on flat graphene sheet, via classical molecular dynamics simulation. Lohrasebi et al. [82] estimated the diffusion coefficient of 4 ×
10
− 9
m
2
s
− 1
for a C60 molecule on a narrow graphene nanoribbon as a quasi-one-dimensional subjected to a temperature gradient, by molecular dynamics simulations. While the motion of a C60 molecule on flat graphene sheet coincides with two-dimensional diffusive Brownian motion, its motion on a narrow graphene nanoribbon coincides with one-dimensional diffusive Brownian motion. Therefore, the one-dimensional diffusion coefficient by Lohrasebi et al. [82] was about one order higher than the two-dimensional diffusion coefficient by Neek-Amal et al. [70]. Since the motions of C60 molecules in this work were restricted in one-dimensional valley, it is reasonable that the diffusion coefficients in this work are higher than those in the previous works [70,82]. We note that Jafary-Zadeh et al. [83] also showed surface Brownian motion of a C60 molecule on a graphene substrate via molecular dynamics simulations, and calculated the temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients. They addressed two distinct regimes realized by changing the temperature of the system. They revealed that the underlying physical origin for this crossover is a mechanism transition of kinetic nanofriction, arising from distinctive ways of interaction between the C60 molecule and the graphene substrate in these two regimes due to the temperature change. Their findings provided insight into surface mass transport and kinetic friction control at the nanoscale.

Basically, a molecule absorbed on a surface moves on the surface, in search of energetic ground sites. In the experimental work by Tamai et al. [2], C60 molecules settled along the nominal terrace of vicinal Cu (221) surface, because the C60 molecules had largest contact area with the Cu surface, and consequently, C60 molecules formed one-dimensional self-assemblies. In general, when the surface does not have the energetic ground sites for the absorbed C60 molecules, the C60 molecules self-assemble into a close-packed monolayer with a hexagonal structure, as mentioned in the Introduction.

When C60 molecules are absorbed onto a flat graphene sheet, C60 molecules form a hexagonally close-packed monolayer structure [69]. Švec et al. [69] found that the island of C60 molecules diffuses fast in the absence of a strong site-specific bonding mechanism. However, the periodically wrinkled graphene surfaces considered in this work can provide a strong site, which takes a C60 molecule, by building up the vdW potential energy barriers along the valley of the wrinkled surface. The C60 molecules falling into this one-dimensional potential well one-dimensionally self-assemble along the narrow valley. Therefore, to form a periodic wrinkled graphene surface with narrow valleys, it is very important to achieve well-ordered one-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules. In a number of papers [63,64,67,84,85], periodic wrinkled graphene flakes or nanoribbons have been fabricated.

For endohedral fullerenes [86], we can also expect well-ordered one-dimensional self-assembly in the same mechanism. Well-ordered one-dimensional endohedral-fullerenes can be applied to quantum computing. Harneit [87] explored the promise and limitations of using endohedral fullerenes N@C60 and P@C60 as quantum information carriers. He discussed the qubits being encoded in the electron spins of nitrogen or phosphorus atoms encapsulated in fullerenes, and quantum operations and readouts being performed by electron-spin resonance (ESR). The coupling between qubits can be mediated by the magnetic dipolar interaction of the encapsulated atoms, and addressing can also be performed by individual tuning of the qubit resonance frequency.

Recently, while Chuvilin et al. [88] addressed the direct transformation of graphene nanoflake into fullerene, Lu et al. [89] showed the ruthenium-assistant transformation of a C60 molecule into graphene nanoflake quantum dot. Considering the well-ordered C60 molecules in this work, it is suggested that well-ordered graphene-quantum-dots arrays can be realized, by transforming C60 molecules into graphene.

In conclusion, we showed that periodically wrinkled graphene sheet can be a pattern to form one-dimensionally well-ordered C60 molecules, via the Monte Carlo method. Since the valleys of the sinusoidal graphene surface provide energetic ground sites for absorbed C60 molecules, their motions seeking stable positions lead to one-dimensional self-assembly. The size of the wrinkles, the density of adsorbed C60 molecules, and the temperature were very important parameters to obtain an one-dimensional C60 molecules array. We estimated high one-dimensional diffusion coefficients of C60 molecules on the wrinkled graphene surface. Our results can provide a possible approach to make a quantum information array based on endohedral fullerenes and a graphene quantum dot array, by transforming C60 molecules to graphene nanoflakes.

Finally, we note that this theoretical study simply addressed the physical mechanism for one-dimensional self-assembly of C60 molecules on the wrinkled graphene surface, and presented only simulation results using the Monte Carlo method. Hence, we anticipate one-dimensional chains of C60 molecules being realized by experimental research groups. In this work, we assumed the ideal graphene surfaces. However, graphene membranes actually have many defects, and such lattice imperfections seriously affect their electronic, thermal, and mechanical properties [90]. So further works should include the disordered arrangement of C60 molecules due to the defects in the graphene membranes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a grant from the Academic Research Program of Korea National University of Transportation in 2013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Practical Mathematical Optimization: An Introduction to Basic Optimization Theory and Classical and New Gradient-Based Algorithms
